segments.s284-g17: همی + گفت is a common Old/epic Persian construction meaning a repeated or ongoing saying — 'kept saying' (همی = still/always; گفت = said). شاهست is a fused form meaning 'is king' (شاه + است, here treated as one word), and پیروزگر is used adjectivally 'victorious / triumphant', so the clause literally reads 'he kept saying: "(that) the king is triumphant."'
segments.s284-g18: همیشه is an adverb 'always'; کلاهش is 'his hat/crown' with the possessive -ش; به + خورشید + بر forms a simple prepositional phrase 'toward/at the sun' (به here = toward/with sense 'towards', خورشید = 'the sun', بر = 'on/upon' used here poetically with the idea of being 'toward' or 'over' the sun). The whole segment reads 'may his crown always be toward the sun.'
segments.s284-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از روز نه ساعت اندر گذشت is a time phrase 'nine hours of the day passed' where از + روز = 'of the day', نه ساعت = 'nine hours', اندر = 'within/in', and گذشت is the past 'passed' (گذشت). The clause is a temporal subordinate: 'When nine hours of the day had passed...'.
segments.s284-g20: ز (poetic form of از) introduces the source/agent 'of the Turks' (ز ترکان). نبد is an archaic negative past of بودن ('was not'); کس here in negative context means 'anyone / no one'. بر آن پهن‌دشت = 'on that broad plain' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, پهن‌دشت = broad plain). Word order is flexible: literal 'From the Turks there was not anyone on that broad plain.'
segments.s284-g21: کسی را is the object 'someone' marked by the object marker را. کجا is an interrogative/adverb here used rhetorically ('how/where' or effectively 'if/when') modifying the verb. پروراند is past 'raised / reared' (caused to be nurtured). به ناز = 'with pampering/indulgence' where به functions like 'by/with' and ناز = pampering/affection. Together the segment conveys a conditional/rhetorical idea: 'if/when someone is reared with pampering.'
segments.s284-g22: برآید is a verbal form meaning 'arises / befalls / comes about' (impersonal or intransitive in usage here). بر او = 'upon him' (بر = upon, او = him), روزگار دراز = 'long time/long fortune' (روزگار = time/fate, دراز = long). The clause literally 'a long time/fortune arises upon him' i.e. 'long fortune will befall him.'
segments.s284-g23: شبیخون ('night-raid' / surprise attack) is the nominal subject, کند is the verb 'does/makes', and گاه شادی is an adverbial inversion 'sometimes joy' (گاه = sometimes, شادی = joy). بر اوی = 'upon him' (بر + او fused as اوی in archaic/poetic form). The poetic word order inverts subject and adverb: 'Sometimes joy makes a surprise attack upon him.'
segments.s284-g24: همی is the habitual/continuous particle ('then/again/always' used here to mark recurring result). خواری و سختی are the things brought ('humiliation and hardship'), آرد is the verb 'brings' (present/habitual form), and به روی = 'to the face' (به = to, روی = face). The clause means 'it brings humiliation and hardship to his face,' i.e. misfortune shows itself openly.
segments.s284-g1: دهم is an ordinal/adverbial here ('the tenth' / 'at the tenth turn') marking sequence. It precedes the name/word برته and sets the temporal/order frame: 'at the tenth [move]'. برته and کهرم are coordinated by با ('with'), so the phrase means 'Barteh with Kohram' (کهرم carries the epithet تیغ‌زن 'sword-striker' after it). The whole segment is a noun phrase giving who acted/appeared at that tenth turn.
segments.s284-g2: This is a verbless/descriptive clause that attributes states to the two named men: دو خونی ('both blood-stained' / 'both bloody') describes their condition. و simply links the next predicate. هر دو سر انجمن literally 'each both head(s) of the assembly' — سر here means 'chief/leader', so the phrase says both were heads/leading men of the gathering.
segments.s284-g3: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here meaning 'continually' or 'indeed' used in narrative to mark ongoing action. آزمودند is the simple past (3rd pl.) of آزمايیدن 'to try/test' meaning 'they tried'. هرگونه جنگ is the object—'every sort/kind of fight'—with هرگونه modifying جنگ.
segments.s284-g4: گرفتند is past tense (3rd pl.) 'they seized/took'. پس here means 'then' linking the sequence. تیغ هندی is a noun phrase 'Indian sword' (تیغ = sword, هندی = Indian/Indian-style). به چنگ is a prepositional phrase literally 'into the grip' or 'in the hand/clutch' (به + چنگ) indicating how they took it.
segments.s284-g5: درفش همایون is a nominal phrase: درفش 'banner/standard' modified by همایون 'auspicious/imperial' (an epithet). به دست اندرون is a locative expression: به + دست ('to/at the hand') with اندرون 'within/inside', so together 'within the hand' (i.e. the banner held in the hand).
segments.s284-g6: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. گفتی here is used as a reporting verb 'you said' (past 2nd singular); the provided lemma senses are nominal, so the verbal sense is not in that list. بجنبد is a present/subjunctive-type form meaning 'would move/tremble' (3rd sing. with ب- prefix), and که introduces the subordinate clause 'that Bisotun' (بیستون as a simile/place).
segments.s284-g7: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn'. بپیچید is past 3rd pl. of پیچیدن with the verbal prefix ب- here meaning 'they twisted/turned' or 'they wound' (narrative past). از او marks the source 'from him'; برته is the object 'a lock/tuft', and روی here indicates location/surface 'on (his) face' or 'from his head/face' depending on context.
segments.s284-g8: یکی functions as 'one (of them)' or 'a certain one'. تیغ زد is a compound: تیغ (sword) + زد (past 'struck') = 'struck with the sword'. بر سر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the head' (بر = on/against, سر = head). و links the clause to ترگ اوی, where ترگ 'rag/shred' is followed by اوی 'his' (possessive). The clause implies his rag/garment was cut/torn by the blow.
segments.s284-g9: که here introduces a result/clause linked to the preceding action; تا means “until” and sets the limit that follows. The phrase سینه کهرم بدو نیم گشت literally orders constituents as ‘breast (سینه) — کهرم — to him (بدو) — half (نیم) — became (گشت)’, so گشت is a past (perfective) verb meaning “became/turned,” and بدو is an indirect-dative ‘to him’ placed before the predicate; the whole segment says that the breast became half (i.e. was halved/partly affected) up to that point.
segments.s284-g10: ز marks source/cause (‘from’ or ‘because of’ the enemy). دل is the noun ‘heart’ which takes the modifier برته (here placed immediately after it in older/poetic word-order instead of an ezāfe). بی‌بیم is a compound adjective/adverb meaning ‘fearless’; گشت is the past verb ‘became.’ The clause thus reads ‘because of the enemy, (his) heart became fearless,’ with برته qualifying دل.
segments.s284-g11: فرود آمد is a compound past tense verb meaning ‘came down’ or ‘dismounted.’ از اسب is the source phrase ‘from the horse.’ The conjunction و links the next clause; او را is the direct object plus the object marker را, and ببست is a past transitive verb here meaning ‘he bound/tied (him).’
segments.s284-g12: بر آن زین توزی is a prepositional phrase: بر ‘on/upon’, آن ‘that’, زین ‘saddle’, with توزی an attributive element (an adjective or name) modifying زین. The conjunction و joins the final clause; خود is emphatic/reflexive ‘himself,’ and برنشست is a past verb meaning ‘sat upon/took his seat’ (perfective past).
segments.s284-g13: برآمد is a past verb meaning ‘rose/arose’ or ‘sprang up.’ به بالا is an adverbial phrase ‘upwards/on high.’ چو is the comparative/simile ‘like/as,’ and شرزه پلنگ is a noun phrase where شرزه (an adjective meaning fierce/rebellious) qualifies پلنگ ‘leopard’; the adjective precedes the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s284-g14: خروشان is an adverbial/participial form ‘roaring’ or ‘bellowing,’ modifying the action that follows. یکی is an indefinite ‘a certain’ (introducing the following noun), تیغ هندی is ‘an Indian sword’ with هندی as an adjectival qualifier, and به چنگ is an idiomatic phrase ‘in (his) grip/claw’ (چنگ = claw/hand here) indicating the instrument/possession.
segments.s284-g15: درفش همایون is a compound noun phrase ‘the auspicious/imperial banner’ (درفش = standard, همایون = auspicious/royal). به دست اندرون is a directional phrase ‘into the (inner) hand’ or ‘into his inner grasp’ (به دست = into the hand, اندرون = inside/within). In context this names the object and the recipient/location.
segments.s284-g16: فگنده is a past participle/archaic perfect of ‘to throw’ (فکندن) meaning ‘having thrown’ or simply ‘thrown.’ بر آن باره is a prepositional/time phrase ‘upon that occasion’ (باره = occasion/instance). کهرم نگون is a noun plus adjective in the older poetic order: کهرم (a name/term) followed by نگون ‘overthrown/downcast’; the verb is implicit/passive sense ‘was overthrown’ with نگون serving as the predicate adjective.
segments.s284-g25: ز + noun + اندر + آرد is a poetic prepositional phrase: ز باد اندر آرد = 'it brings in/from the wind'. آرد is 3rd singular present 'brings'. دهدمان is a present form meaning 'gives us' (دهد + مان = 'gives us') and follows as a second verb; به دم ('for/by breath') here means 'for a moment' or 'a moment's breath.' Word order is poetic (prepositional phrase then verbs then adverbial).
segments.s284-g26: همی is a habitual/adverbial marker 'continually/always' modifying the verb. داد here is a noun 'justice' (object), and خوانیم is 1st‑person plural present/habitual 'we call/cry (out)' (here 'call for'); و simply connects clauses; پیدا is an adjective 'apparent/visible' that predicates over ستم 'oppression' (i.e. 'oppression was apparent').
segments.s284-g27: به تورانیان بر indicates direction/target (به ... بر = 'upon/against the Turanians'); چنان شد که is the idiom 'it so happened that'. آن جنگ شوم = 'that battle (جنگ) [was] ill‑omened' — شوم here functions adjectivally 'ill‑omened' rather than as a 1st‑person verb.
segments.s284-g28: به آوردگه کردن is a light‑verb construction 'to make for/to the place (آوردگه)' — کردن is the auxiliary 'to make/do'; آهنگ کردن = 'to set one's intention/aim' (آهنگ = intention/aim). شوم again modifies آهنگ as 'ill‑omened' (an attributive adjective), so the phrase means roughly 'I turned/laid an ill‑omened intent toward the battlefield.'
segments.s284-g29: چنان شد که = 'it so happened that' (idiomatic). پیران here is the subject 'the elders' and ز توران سپاه forms a genitive/attributive group 'of the Turanian army' (ز = 'from/of'), so 'the elders of the Turanian host'.
segments.s284-g30: سواری is the direct object 'horsemen/riders'; ندید is past negative 'did not see' (ن‑ prefix + دید); اندر آوردگاه is a locative phrase 'in/at the battlefield' (اندر = 'in', آوردگاه = 'battlefield'). Note the object precedes the verb in this classical word order.
segments.s284-g31: روان‌ها is the plural noun 'souls/spirits'. گسسته is a past‑participle/adjective 'separated, severed' predicating of روان‌ها. ز تنشان is a genitive/instrumental 'from their bodies' (تنشان = 'their bodies'). به تیغ uses به with an instrumental sense 'by/with the sword' (به + instrument) — here 'by the sword'.
segments.s284-g32: جهان را is topicalized/definite object 'the world' (را marks the noun). تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you'; گفتی is the 2nd‑person past 'you said' (past tense verb). نیامد دریغ is a small clause 'no regret came' — نیامد = 'did not come' (or 'did not arise'), دریغ = 'pity/regret'; together 'no pity/regret came'.

sentences.s284-s9: He kept saying, "The king is victorious; may his crown always be toward the sun."
sentences.s284-s10: When nine hours of the day had passed, not one of the Turks was on that broad plain.
sentences.s284-s11: If someone is reared in pampering, long fortune will befall him.
sentences.s284-s12: Sometimes joy makes a surprise attack upon him; it brings humiliation and hardship to his face.
sentences.s284-s1: At the tenth [turn], Barteh together with Kohram the sword-striker were bloodied, and both were chiefs of the assembly.
sentences.s284-s2: They tried every kind of combat; then they seized the Indian sword in their grasp.
sentences.s284-s3: You said that the auspicious banner within your hand would sway like Bisotun.
sentences.s284-s4: One by one they twisted away a lock from him; one struck his head with a sword and his rag (garment) [was torn].
sentences.s284-s5: So that until his breast had become half for him, from the enemy his heart became fearless.
sentences.s284-s6: He dismounted from the horse and bound him to it, and he himself sat up on that saddle.
sentences.s284-s7: He sprang up on high like a furious leopard, roaring, an Indian sword in his grasp.
sentences.s284-s8: The auspicious standard he (or someone) cast into the inner hand, and upon that occasion Kahrm was overthrown.
sentences.s284-s13: From the wind it brings (to us), giving us but a moment's breath; we continually cried for justice, and oppression became evident.
sentences.s284-s14: That ill‑fated battle against the Turanians — I set my ill‑omened intent toward the battlefield.
sentences.s284-s15: It came to pass that the elders of the Turanian host saw no horsemen in the battlefield.
sentences.s284-s16: Their souls were severed from their bodies by the sword; as you said, no regret came to the world.
